howItWorks.intro: The true cost of a 3D print includes more than just filament. This calculator accounts for all major cost factors to give you an accurate per-print cost.
rules: Material cost: filament weight (g) × cost per gram from your spool price
Electricity: printer wattage × print time × your electricity rate
Depreciation: printer cost spread over its estimated lifespan in print hours
howItWorks.outro: Add your desired markup percentage to set a fair selling price. Most sellers use 100-300% markup depending on complexity and market. Don't forget to factor in design time and post-processing labor.

faq.question: How much does filament cost per 3D print?
faq.answer: At $25/kg for PLA filament, a typical small print using 30g of material costs about $0.75 in filament. Medium prints (100g) cost about $2.50, and large prints (300g+) can cost $7.50 or more. Specialty filaments like PETG, ABS, or carbon fiber cost more per kg.

faq.question: How much electricity does a 3D printer use?
faq.answer: Most FDM 3D printers draw 100-300 watts. A typical 200W printer running for 4 hours at $0.12/kWh costs about $0.10 in electricity. Electricity is usually the smallest cost component — material and time are much bigger factors.

faq.question: How should I price 3D prints to sell?
faq.answer: Calculate your total cost (material + electricity + depreciation + labor) and apply a markup of 100-300%. Simple prints might use 100% markup, while complex or custom designs can command 200-400%. Factor in design time, post-processing (sanding, painting), and shipping costs.

faq.question: How do I find the filament weight for my print?
faq.answer: Your slicer software (Cura, PrusaSlicer, etc.) estimates filament weight before printing. It's shown in the print summary along with print time and filament length. For more accuracy, weigh the finished print on a kitchen scale.

faq.question: What is printer depreciation?
faq.answer: Depreciation spreads the cost of your printer across its useful life. If a $300 printer lasts 2,000 print hours, each hour costs $0.15 in depreciation. This accounts for wear, maintenance, and eventual replacement — an often-overlooked cost in pricing.

faq.question: Does print speed affect cost?
faq.answer: Yes. Faster prints use less electricity and depreciation time but may sacrifice quality. A print that takes 4 hours instead of 8 saves roughly half the electricity and depreciation costs, though material usage stays the same.

howWeCalculate.intro: This 3d print cost calculator uses established formulas and industry-standard data to provide accurate estimates.
steps: Enter your specific values into the calculator fields above
Our algorithm applies the relevant formulas using your inputs
Results are calculated instantly in your browser — nothing is sent to a server
Review the detailed breakdown to understand how each factor affects your result
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These calculations are estimates based on standard formulas. For critical decisions, always consult a qualified professional.

whenToUse.intro: This 3d print cost calculator is designed for anyone who needs quick, reliable estimates without complex spreadsheets or professional consultations.
scenarios: When you need a quick estimate before committing to a purchase or project
When comparing different options or scenarios side by side
When planning a budget and need to understand potential costs
When you want to verify a quote or estimate you've received from a professional

sections.title: What Is the 3D Print Cost Calculator?
sections.body: The 3D Print Cost Calculator is a free online tool designed for makers and 3D printing enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the 3D printing and additive manufacturing space. By entering your filament used, filament cost per kg, print time, you get instant results including total cost, suggested sell price, material cost. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting total cost right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In 3D printing and additive manufacturing, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by makers and 3D printing enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your prints with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your filament used and need to find the right total cost. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Filament Used (grams)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your filament used — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: filament cost per kg, print time, electricity rate, printer wattage, printer cost, printer lifespan, markup %. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your total cost, suggested sell price, material cost. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your filament used before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both total cost and suggested sell price — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your prints.
If you're unsure about your markup %, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
